skill.name: git-workflow
skill.description: Manage Git workflows including commits, branches, merges, and collaboration. Use when working with Git repositories, creating commits, managing branches, or resolving conflicts.
skill.summary: Comprehensive Git workflow assistant for branch management, commits, merges, and team collaboration with best practices.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: This skill is a documentation-only guide for Git workflows. Static analysis flagged 77 shell command patterns, 6 URLs, and 14 crypto patterns, but all are false positives. The detected patterns are Markdown code blocks (```bash) and inline code markers (`command`) used for documentation formatting, not executable code. URLs are reference links to official Git documentation. No actual security risks exist.

content.value_statement: Struggling with Git branches, merges, and conflicts? This skill provides clear, step-by-step guidance for all Git operations with best practices for team collaboration.
actual_capabilities: Generate conventional commit messages following industry standards
Guide branch naming conventions and creation workflows
Provide step-by-step merge and rebase instructions
Help resolve Git conflicts with clear explanations
Support stash, cherry-pick, and bisect operations
Configure Git aliases and user settings
limitations: Cannot execute Git commands directly - provides guidance only
Requires user to run commands in their local terminal
Does not integrate with GitHub or GitLab APIs for PR creation
Cannot access remote repositories without user authentication

use_cases.title: New Developer Onboarding
use_cases.description: Help junior developers learn proper Git workflows with guided examples for branching, committing, and merging code safely.

use_cases.title: Team Workflow Standardization
use_cases.description: Establish consistent commit message formats and branch naming conventions across your development team.

use_cases.title: Conflict Resolution Assistance
use_cases.description: Get clear step-by-step guidance when facing complex merge conflicts during feature integration.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Commit Message
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me write a commit message for changes that add user authentication with JWT tokens to my application.

prompt_templates.title: Branch Creation and Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to start working on a new feature for the shopping cart. What branch should I create and how?

prompt_templates.title: Merge Conflict Resolution
prompt_templates.prompt: I'm getting conflicts when rebasing my feature branch onto main. Walk me through resolving them step by step.

prompt_templates.title: Interactive Rebase for Cleanup
prompt_templates.prompt: I have 5 messy commits on my feature branch. Help me squash and reorganize them before merging to main.

output_examples.input: Write a commit message for adding login functionality
output_examples.output:
feat(auth): implement user login system

- Add login form component with validation
- Create authentication API endpoint
- Integrate session management middleware
- Add unit tests for login flow

Closes #156

output_examples.input: How do I undo my last commit but keep my changes?
output_examples.output:
Use: git reset --soft HEAD~1

This removes the commit but keeps all changes staged. If you want to unstage them too, use: git reset HEAD~1

best_practices: Write small, focused commits that change one thing at a time
Use conventional commit format: type(scope): subject
Always pull latest changes before starting new work
Never commit directly to the main branch
Delete feature branches after merging to keep history clean
anti_patterns: Committing with messages like 'fix' or 'update' - be specific about what changed
Using git push --force without --force-with-lease - can overwrite teammate work
Committing large batches of unrelated changes - makes review and rollback difficult
Ignoring merge conflicts and forcing pushes - leads to lost code

faq.question: What is the difference between git merge and git rebase?
faq.answer: Merge creates a new commit combining two branches, preserving history. Rebase rewrites commit history by replaying commits on top of another branch, creating a linear history. Use merge for shared branches, rebase for local feature cleanup.

faq.question: How do I undo a commit that was already pushed?
faq.answer: Use git revert <commit-hash> to create a new commit that undoes the changes. This is safer than reset for shared branches because it preserves history and does not require force push.

faq.question: What should I do if I committed to the wrong branch?
faq.answer: Create a new branch from your current state with git branch correct-branch. Then reset the wrong branch with git reset --hard HEAD~1. Finally, checkout your correct branch.

faq.question: How often should I pull changes from main?
faq.answer: Pull from main at least daily when working on long-running features. This reduces merge conflicts by keeping your branch current with team changes.

faq.question: What is git stash and when should I use it?
faq.answer: Git stash temporarily saves uncommitted changes so you can switch branches. Use it when you need to quickly switch context but are not ready to commit your work.

faq.question: What are conventional commits and why use them?
faq.answer: Conventional commits use a standard format: type(scope): description. Types include feat, fix, docs, refactor, test, and chore. This creates clear history and enables automated changelog generation.
